author: @NorthernKitty
text: I'm honestly surprised they didn't already make it to operate on 12V or 24V, and looking at comments below I hadn't even considered that they might not even have current limiting built-in to the panel.  On that note, the market for plug-n-play 12V or 24V DC lighting fixtures seems wide-open - I really can't find much on-line beyond really basic recessed lights, dome lights and lighting strips.  I would think people would love to have a wider range of more decorative and feature-rich options to install in their off-grid cabins, RVs or van conversions.  😊

text: Quick tip. Multiply the number of led in series by 3.2 volts. Measure the current when they are cold and run at a constant current so they dont thermal runaway.

author: @paranoiia8
text:
Nice video.
But does it matter what way we put that clear layer? I remember I had one of that and in one side it was smooth and other had those diffuse lines. Could we lose eg brightness level if we put it other way around or it doesn't matter?

replies.author: @zaprodk
replies.text: It does matter if it's only pattered on one side or in a specific pattern, so make sure to put it back the same way it came out. simple.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes mate it matters - but I thought I said in the video to put them back the same way - if I didn't make the obvious I am sorry - if you do put them back the wrong way you will know - so just swap them around until you get it back the same way - there aren't that many combinations if you get it mixed up it's not the end of the world - but better if you just stack them as they came out and put them back that way

replies.author: @paranoiia8
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering its just when you have few of them and you put them on the side and leave for few days you just dont remember with side they where :]

author: @benthere8051
text:
Thanks again, Robert. Your recent large cell battery video is obviously a good choice for solar backup. But there is a large community of people that want and need to have a backup power supply in case of power outages. A set of your batteries combined with an inverter could be used in place of a big gas-powered generator in case of an emergency.
Such a system could be kept topped-off by the local power system and could kick in in milliseconds in the case of a power company interruption.
Your batteries could be as good as a big generator in a short-term outage.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they could indeed mate

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
That's not a bad little project actually. The only problem with wiring the LEDs straight to a battery is they have a pretty flat voltage over a broad range of current, meaning if you increase the voltage slightly you can easily overload the LEDs with too much current. Also, as the battery gets discharged your light will get a lot dimmer when the batteries still have a lot of juice left in them.

As a simple fix to the first issue, you could just include a current-limiting resistor (1 or 2 ohms, 4Watt) which will take up the extra voltage. However, I'd suggest getting a cheap dc-dc boost converter from Aliexpress where you set a constant current into the LEDs. That way you could just run your 30V panel with a 12V battery. If you do that, be aware of overdischarging the battery.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ah that explains something to me - cheers mate

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: My thoughts exactly. Good project can always be a bit better with little effort.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering No problem mate. I'm glad to be able to say something useful for once lol.

replies.author: @basselq
replies.text: There must be some sort of protection circuit for the battery as well preventing it for over discharging

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: @basselq Yeah. Lithium batteries usually have a thing called a BMS which include a low-voltage cutoff. For lead acid, it looks like you get adjustable low voltage cutoff modules on Aliexpress with a relay on board. That's probably what I'd get along with the boost circuit so you just need a single 12V Lead Acid battery to drive even the original 36-48V panels.

author: @carpetcrawlerunited
text: should really use a current limiting circuit mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think so mate - voltage limit is good enough to my mind - why would I use current limiting? Bear in mind I am not being pig headed - I just wonder why you would want it as essentially can't a simple current limiting circuit be little more than a diode and resistor network - which is what this is

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: It looks like the wires are pretty thin, so there'd probably be a good 1/2 ohm resistance in-line. I think Rob can be glad the LED voltage is quite high, so they manage. I'm sure they would blow up eventually though.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Jodie explained it to me mate - understood and thanks for the tip

replies.author: @carpetcrawlerunited
replies.text: @ThisRandomUsername thats still about 60/65watts the battery will try to drive into those led strings.theres a reason why we use constant current drivers with leds,not constant voltage

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: @carpetcrawlerunited You're right. I figured since they didn't blow up straight away (as you'll see in my comment before *edit: I mean my top-level comment*), the LEDs must be at least fairly well matched with the voltage of the batteries, so adding a 1 or 2 ohm resistor should be good enough if it could handle the current.
